entries.title: What's holding up the Gaza stabilization mission?
entries.summary: A multinational stabilization mission is supposed to ensure the reconstruction of Gaza. Without political consensus among Israel, Hamas and the Arab states, its implementation remains uncertain.
entries.content:
Palestinian Territories

What's holding up the Gaza stabilization mission?

July 22, 2026

A multinational stabilization mission is supposed to ensure the reconstruction of Gaza. Without political consensus among Israel, Hamas and the Arab states, its implementation remains uncertain.

Morocco recently signed an agreement to participate ​in the International Stabilization Force (ISF) in Gaza.

This agreement "reflects the shared determination to contribute, through concrete ​humanitarian and security actions, to the ​establishment of a climate of peace and security in the region," Morocco's state news agency MAP reported in July. According to the report, Morocco's contribution includes military personnel, police and gendarmerie forces and a field hospital.

Morocco is the first Arab country to join the planned international security mission for the Gaza Strip. Indonesia, Kazakhstan, Kosovo and Albania are also part of the force, while Jordan and Egypt have agreed to supporting roles only.

However, it remains to be seen whether the ISF can ever be deployed on the scale originally envisioned by its initiators.

The force, which is linked to the "Board of Peace" established by US President Donald Trump, is intended to do far more than simply secure humanitarian aid: It's seen as the security cornerstone of the planned post-war order for Gaza.

No traditional UN peacekeeping mission

The International Stabilization Force is tasked with securing the reconstruction process, protecting humanitarian aid deliveries, and training new Palestinian security forces. In the long term, it is intended to support a political transition.

However, in contrast to traditional United Nations peacekeeping missions, it will be organized outside the usual UN command structures even though the UN authorized the force. This structural difference makes the ISF one of the most ambitious — and one of the most challenging — international projects for the Gaza Strip.

"The involvement of international actors requires at least a minimum level of political stability," Gil Murciano, executive director of the Israeli Institute for Regional Foreign Policies, told DW. The real problem, he said, is not the mission itself, but the lack of a political framework in which it needs to operate.

Marcus Schneider, head of Germany's political Friedrich Ebert Foundation's Regional Project for Peace and Security in the Middle East in Beirut, is also skeptical about the mission's prospects for success.

"Israel wants to secure military freedom of action, while potential troop-contributing countries are primarily interested in a traditional peacekeeping mission," he told DW, adding that "these differing expectations pretty much rule each other out."

This view is echoed by the International Peace Institute (IPI). According to a recent analysis, a stable, legitimate Palestinian administration is needed first; otherwise, the ISF risks becoming yet another international transitional project.

Israel, Hamas, and the Arab states still differ on Gaza's future

In particular, Israel, Hamas, and the Arab states have differing views on what role the ISF should play. From Israel's perspective, security is the top priority.

"The Israeli government has made its position very clear: as long as Hamas does not agree to disarm, the reconstruction of Gaza should not begin," Murciano said. This ties reconstruction to a political condition that has not yet been agreed upon.

In Schneider's assessment, the actors involved are pursuing different political goals. While Israel wants to preserve its military freedom of action, many potential troop-contributing countries expect a clearly defined mandate with manageable risks. However, these expectations are currently difficult to reconcile.

The stance of Hamas — which is designated as a terrorist organization by the US and the EU, among others — further complicates the planning. Although the organization has signaled a willingness to withdraw from the civilian administration of Gaza, it has so far refused to fully disarm.

From Israel's perspective, this means that a key prerequisite for the mission's deployment remains unmet; at the same time, many international actors argue that reconstruction and political stabilization should not wait until all security issues have been resolved.

The role of the Arab states in Gaza

Arab states play a key role in Gaza's future as they are considered the most important financial backers for reconstruction. They could also lend the mission the necessary regional legitimacy. However, many governments want to avoid sending troops and billions in aid to Gaza without a solid political roadmap.

Morocco's commitment is seen as an important signal, even though it does not change the fact that other major regional powers have so far remained cautious.

The Washington-based think tank Arab Center Washington DC highlights this dilemma. While many Arab states are, in principle, willing to support the reconstruction of Gaza, participation in an international stabilization mission would be possible for them only within the framework of a credible political process, a report stated.

Gaza's reconstruction as a prerequisite for security

The real challenge lies in solving this stalemate, predicts Murciano. "Reconstruction itself is an essential component of security," he said. Anyone who separates the two processes risks further entrenching the political stalemate, he added.

Whether the ISF will ever be able to assume its intended role remains uncertain. While planning is well advanced, the mission still lacks the political conditions needed for deployment.

This article was originally published in German.

entries.title: Congo: Ebola deaths increase to 1,000
entries.summary: Authorities in Congo have said the latest outbreak of the Ebola virus has killed 999 people since mid-May. That fatality rate is far higher than in previous outbreaks. No vaccines or treatments exist for the new strain.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

Authorities in Congo have said the latest outbreak of the Ebola virus has killed 999 people since mid-May. That fatality rate is far higher than in previous outbreaks. No vaccines or treatments exist for the new strain.

The World Health Organization (WHO) on Wednesday published an overview of new data documenting the rapid spread of the current Ebola outbreak in Congo.

Democratic Republic of Congo Ministry of Health data documenting cases traced between May 15 and July 19 shows that 2,473 people in Congo and neighboring Uganda have been infected by the latest Bundibugyo strain of the virus, with 999 documented deaths.

The spread — the fastest ever recorded for Ebola — has continued unabated as no vaccines or treatments exist for this new strain of the virus.

Until now, the deadliest outbreak — which raged from 2013 to 2016 and infected 28,000 people, 11,000 of whom died — took some eight months to reach the grim 1,000 death mark.

Ebola spreading faster than officials can track

Health officials in Congo say some 80% of new cases have emerged through unknown transmission chains, suggesting it is spreading far faster than authorities can track.

Overall, 82% of contacts linked to confirmed cases are being traced by authorities. This is well below the 90%-95% rate WHO officials say is needed to contain the virus.

The highly contagious Ebola virus is spread via bodily fluids, such as blood, semen and vomit, and is often fatal.

To date, infections in the central African nation of 116 million have been recorded in five of Congo's 26 provinces. The epicenter of the current outbreak lies in Ituri province, which borders Uganda and South Sudan.

Healthcare workers forced to flee

The severity of the situation is being compounded by attacks on health workers as well as the generally poor working conditions that these individuals face.

Many healthcare workers have been forced to flee affected areas in fear for their own safety, and others say they have not been paid in months.

Speaking in Ghana on Tuesday, Director-General of the Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention Jean Kaseya told attendees at the Africa Health Summit that international efforts to stop the spread of the virus must be intensified.

"If we don't stop this outbreak, it could become one of the worst ebola outbreaks the world has ever documented," Kaseya said.

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

Edited by: Sean Sinico

entries.title: Can India become the next semiconductor hub?
entries.summary: India is investing billions to build a domestic semiconductor industry as major industrial powers seek to diversify supply chains beyond Taiwan.
entries.content:
China

Can India become the next semiconductor hub?

July 22, 2026

India is investing billions to build a domestic semiconductor industry as major industrial powers seek to diversify supply chains beyond Taiwan.

More than 90% of the world's most advanced semiconductor chips are made in Taiwan, an island at the center of the ongoing rivalry between the US and China.

Any disruption to Taiwan's chip industry would likely send shockwaves through the global economy, with modern semiconductors used to make everything from smartphones and electric vehicles (EVs) to aircraft and military systems.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, chip shortages forced automakers to halt production — exposing how dependent the world had become on a handful of manufacturers in East Asia.

The result has been a global race to build more resilient semiconductor supply chains. The US, EU, Japan and South Korea have all launched multi-billion-dollar programs to attract chipmakers.

India is now mounting its biggest push yet to become part of that network and enter the global arena as the next electronics powerhouse.

India's chipmaking ambitions

Last week, the Indian government approved a $13.2 billion (€11.4 billion) semiconductor program labeled Semicon 2.0. The project builds on the $9 billion India Semiconductor Mission (ISM) launched in 2021 and aims to develop the entire chip ecosystem — from chip design and fabrication to advanced packaging, specialty materials, equipment manufacturing and workforce training.

Thousands of engineers working for companies such as Nvidia, AMD, Qualcomm and Texas Instruments already design some of the world's most sophisticated chips from research centers in the Indian cities of Bengaluru, Hyderabad and Noida.

But analysts warn India has been lagging behind in large-scale manufacturing. In the semiconductor industry, this is the area that brings both economic value and strategic importance.

"India already has the expertise to design sophisticated semiconductor chips," said V Kamakoti, director of the Indian Institute of Technology in the city of Madras.

"Indian engineers working for both domestic companies and multinational firms have successfully designed and delivered complex chips," Kamakoti told DW. "The bigger challenge is manufacturing them."

So far, India's progress has been concentrated in chip packaging and testing rather than manufacturing the chips themselves.

Kamakoti said the next step is building a domestic fabrication ecosystem that can turn those design strengths into large-scale production.

Hopes of a $100 billion market by 2030

Micron Technology, a US memory-chip maker, Kaynes Semicon and CG Semi, two Indian semiconductor assembly and packaging companies, have begun commercial production under the first phase of India's semiconductor program.

India's Minister for Electronics and Information Technology Ashwini Vaishnaw says the next phase is intended to move more of the semiconductor value chain into India.

"Convincing global companies is no longer that difficult," said ISM chief executive Amitesh Kumar Sinha, pointing to the 12 approved fabrication and packaging projects, three of which are already in commercial production.

The government wants to build a domestic semiconductor market worth between $100 billion and $110 billion by 2030 and meet up to three-quarters of India's electronics demand through locally designed and manufactured chips.

A costly industrial leap

Becoming a major chip manufacturer, however, is one of the toughest industrial challenges any country can take on.

A leading-edge fabrication plant can cost more than $20 billion, requires uninterrupted electricity, vast quantities of ultra-pure water and an extensive network of suppliers producing specialized chemicals, silicon wafers and precision equipment.

Kamakoti argues that India should first prove it can manufacture mature 28-nanometer (28 nm) chips reliably at scale before attempting the most advanced 3 nm processors used in high-end smartphones and AI systems.

"28 nm [chips] will meet the majority of India's needs. Once that succeeds, we can gradually move to more advanced technologies," he said.

Riding the AI boom

India's ambitions are being helped by forces beyond its control.

In a time of geopolitical tensions and the rapid growth of AI, companies are rethinking their supply chains and demand for semiconductors is expected to rise sharply over the coming decade.

Sambit Sahu, a semiconductor industry veteran with more than three decades of experience at Qualcomm and Intel, says India must build capabilities across the semiconductor value chain from chip design and intellectual property to fabrication and manufacturing.

"The rapid growth of artificial intelligence will drive demand for semiconductors for years to come, creating a window of opportunity for India," Sahu told DW.

"Building a semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem will be expensive and technically challenging, but with sustained investment and supportive policies, India could emerge as an important manufacturing hub over the next decade."

However, not everyone is convinced India can narrow the manufacturing gap quickly.

A senior semiconductor industry executive, who preferred to stay anonymous, says India is likely to remain stronger in chip design than fabrication for the foreseeable future.

"India has built world-class capabilities in chip design, but manufacturing is a different challenge," he said. "The biggest opportunities in the near term are in packaging, testing and mature-node manufacturing."

Semicon 2.0 marks India's most ambitious attempt yet to enter the semiconductor race.

Whether it succeeds will depend less on the billions India has pledged and more on its ability to turn those commitments into factories, skilled workers and a competitive manufacturing ecosystem.

Edited by: Keith Walker

entries.title: France: Minister quits over pesticide bill — but is staying
entries.summary: Environment Minister Monique Barbut submitted her resignation in protest over the reintroduction of pesticides that scientists say harm human health as well as bees. But President Emmanuel Macron refused to accept it.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

Environment Minister Monique Barbut submitted her resignation in protest over the reintroduction of pesticides that scientists say harm human health as well as bees. But President Emmanuel Macron refused to accept it.

French Minister of Ecological Transition Monique Barbut announced Wednesday that she would "submit her resignation today" to President Emmanuel Macron in protest to the reintroduction of harmful pesticides.

However, Macron did not accept her resignation, and Barbut will stay in her post due to the "urgency to address the consequences of climate change," her team said in comments carried by the AFP news agency.

Government spokesperson Maud Bregeon later said that Barbut will stay in office.

What Barbut said about her resignation

In a post on LinkedIn, Barbut wrote that "contrary to the commitments that had been made to me, the government prevented any debate on the removal of this measure," adding that there had been "one environmental setback too many," since was appointed last October.

"Politics is not my world, and if this is what politics is about, it definitely never will be," said the former head of WWF France and the United Nations Convention to Combat Desertification (UNCCD).

What is in France's pesticide bill?

Barbut's statement comes just after lawmakers appoved a bill that will allow the pesticides acetamiprid and flupyradifurone to come back into use. They have been banned in France since 2018 and 2019, respectively.

Scientists say that the chemicals are dangerous to human health and are harmful to the bee populations that make agriculture possible in the first place.

Proponents of the bill argued that France's beet, apple, and hazlenut farmers need the pesticides to compete against their EU counterparts.

The substances are not banned in the EU but some member states limit their use.

Edited by: Rob Turner

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Germany news: Prosecutors search Deutsche Bank headquarters
entries.summary: Officials are reportedly investigating fraudulent tax claims made by the bank's Postbank subsidiary between 2008 and 2010. Meanwhile, the story of Timmy the whale continues with new DNA results. Follow DW for more.
entries.content:
Published July 22, 2026last updated July 22, 2026

Officials are reportedly investigating fraudulent tax claims made by the bank's Postbank subsidiary between 2008 and 2010. Meanwhile, the story of Timmy the whale continues with new DNA results. Follow DW for more.

Skip next section What you need to know

What you need to know

  • Deutsche Bank has confirmed its headquarters in Frankfurt was searched Wednesday
  • Economy and Energy Minister Reiche said there are no further state measures planned to lower gas prices
  • New DNA results could offer more information on the death of Timmy the Whale
  • German FM Wadephul is off to the Abuja, Nigeria on the next leg of his Africa trip

Here is a round-up of the top headlines from and about Germany on Wednesday, July 22, 2026:

Tired of missing our real-time updates? Click here to add us as a Preferred Source on Google. Then tap the "Star" or "Preferred" to keep DW News at the top of your feed.

Skip next section Munich court upholds ban on night-time sale of beer in university district

Munich court upholds ban on night-time sale of beer in university district

A court in the Bavarian capital of Munich upheld the city's recently imposed ban on the sale of alcoholic drinks at to-go kiosks in the area around its main Ludwig Maximilian University (LMU) campus.

Beer to go is a favorite of German students, who in summer months often sit in squares and parks with friends and drink rather than go to bars. However, long-term residents of the district began to complain about noise, public urination and broken glass.

The court did agree, however, that the €5,000 ($5,700) fine was excessive and struck it down. A new fine has yet to be agreed upon.

Several suits to overturn the ban are still pending. They have been brought by local kiosk owners who said the prohibition on selling to-go alcoholic beverages has seriously hurt their business.

Skip next section Conservative parliamentary leader replaced after surrogacy scandal

Conservative parliamentary leader replaced after surrogacy scandal

German Chancellor Friedrich Merz has nominated his chief of staff, Thorsten Frei, to succeed Jens Spahn as head of his Christian conservatives (CDU/CSU) bloc in parliament.

Spahn resigned last week following the announcement that he and his husband had become fathers via a surrogate mother in the United States. As former health minister, Spahn consistently voted along party lines to keep surrogacy illegal in Germany and once made comments about his moral qualms about people using a "rented womb."

His perceived flouting of German laws drew swift condemnation from across the country's political spectrum, including his own party.

Frei will have to be officially elected by members of the CDU in the Bundestag, alongside those of its Bavarian sister party, the CSU. That is expected to take place next week. Frei has already received the approval of CSU leader Markus Söder as well as that of Merz.

Skip next section READ: Hitler's birth house opens as police station

READ: Hitler's birth house opens as police station

The quaint house where Adolf Hitler was born in Braunau, Austria opened as a police station today.

This comes after an Austrian government commission offered suggestions on how to appropriately use the house, and the state poured millions of euros into rennovations. But in Braunau, reactions to the house's new function as mixed, and some see a missed opportunity.

DW has the full story on how Austria turned Hitler's birth house into a police station here. 

Skip next section DNA results linked to Timmy the Whale expected

DNA results linked to Timmy the Whale expected

The dramatic tale surrounding the humpback whale known as "Timmy" is set to continue today.

Experts are expected to provide DNA results on whether a dead humpback whale found floating off Denmark’s coast on May 14 match samples taken from Timmy. The findings will be presented at a press conference in Hamburg this afternoon.

Danish authorities previously identified the dead whale as Timmy based on a tracking device attached to its fin.

The story of Timmy captivated Germany for months earlier this year.

After repeatedly stranding along Germany’s Baltic Sea coast, the whale spent weeks trapped on a sandbank in Wismar Bay in April, before being rescued by a private rescue initiative which guided the marine mammal out to sea in a specially converted freight barge.

The rescue effort was accompanied by heated debate among scientists, government officials, the public and the private initiative behind the rescue operation over whether the weakened and ailing whale should be left to die in peace or whether rescuers should continue their efforts to save it.

Skip next section Deutsche Bank headquarters searched by prosecutors

Deutsche Bank headquarters searched by prosecutors

Finance giant Deutsche Bank has confirmed that prosecutors searched its main offices in Frankfurt on Wednesday.

Officials are reportedly investigating fraudulent tax claims made by the bank's subsidiary Postbank between 2008 and 2010.

"Deutsche Bank is being searched as a third party in this matter and we are cooperating fully," the group said without elaborating.

The investigation relates to the practice of making significant stock trades around dividend payout days, a scheme that flourished during the financial crisis that began in 2007. Authorities say the practice amounted to tax fraud.

Deutsche Bank is no stranger to controversy. A recent release of Epstein files show the late financier and convicted sex offender had up to 40 accounts with Deutsche Bank.  The bank was also raided earlier this year over a money laundering probe.

Skip next section German Foreign Minister Wadephul travels to Abuja, Nigeria continuing Africa tour

German Foreign Minister Wadephul travels to Abuja, Nigeria continuing Africa tour

German Foreign Minister Johann Wadephul is traveling Abuja, Nigeria on Wednesday, as part of his third tour of African countries this year, which has already included Mauritania. 

His visit to Nigeria's capital comes after he arrived in Lagos yesterday, the country's largest city and economic hub.

In his tour of Africa's most populous country, Wadephul is being accompanied by a number of German business representatives. Economic cooperation is a key issue in talks with Nigeria, which is Germany's second largest trading partner in Sub-Saharan Africa.

"Anyone who isn’t present in Africa today risks missing out on crucial opportunities," said Wadephul on German businesses and investments in the region.

Cooperation in the field of critical minerals and energy also been a priority during the visit as Wadephul stressed the need for Germany to diversify its supply chains. Nigeria is Africa's largest exporter of oil.

In Abuja, Wadephul is expected to speak with his Nigerian counterpart Bianca Odumegwu-Ojukwu and visit the secretariat of and Economic Community of West African States (ECOWAS). Talks there are expected to focus on multilateral engagement as well as security issues, in a country that has long battled militancy and violence in the northeastern region. 

Skip next section No new measures against high gas prices — Energy Minister Reiche

No new measures against high gas prices — Energy Minister Reiche

Economy and Energy Minister Katherina Reiche said that no additional state measures were planned to combat high gas prices.

In May, the federal government had reduced tax on gasoline and diesel by 17 cents in order to combat the rise in the price of fuel at the pump due to the Iran war. But the tax reduction expired at the end of June.

Since then, prices have risen quickly, fueled also by the resumption of hostilities in the Middle East, and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz. Currently, the average price of one liter of gasoline stands at roughly €2.20 ($9.50 per gallon), up from €1.83 ($7.78 per gallon), from when before the war began, according to public broadcaster NDR.

"With the fuel price cap, we implemented a very, very expensive measure. It cost us many billions of euros," Reiche told the German media network RTL. “Those funds are not currently available in the federal budget."

Reiche, of the center-right Christian Democratic Union's (CDU), also rejected the idea of a price cap for gas, proposed by some members of the center-left Social Democrats, the junior members of the governing coalition.

"No further measures are currently planned, nor are any actually in the works," said Reiche.

Skip next section Welcome to our coverage

Welcome to our coverage

Guten Morgen from DW’s newsroom!

Bonn has been fortuitously blessed with mild summer temperatures, which makes bringing you the news of the day all the more delightful.

German Economy and Energy Minister Katherina Reiche Reiche has announced that there were no additional measures planned to combat high gas prices in the wake of the Iran War, after a tax relief expired at the end of June.

The results of a DNA examination linked to the humpback whale "Timmy" are expected this afternoon. Timmy was freed after multiple strandings off the German Baltic Sea coast this spring, only to be found dead off Denmark in May.

The results could offer more information if the whale carcass found in Denmark is indeed Timmy's.

Germany's Foreign Minister Wadephul is traveling to the Nigerian capital Abuja today, as talks in Africa's most populous country center on business, resource and security cooperation.

entries.title: Will surge in German business bankruptcies hit growth?
entries.summary: Company insolvencies in Germany are rising sharply. Is this a sign of deeper economic weakness, or just a market correction that could ultimately benefit future growth?
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

Company insolvencies in Germany are rising sharply. Is this a sign of deeper economic weakness, or just a market correction that could ultimately benefit future growth?

How bad are Germany's economic problems? One commonly used indicator is the number of company insolvencies and recent figures have produced worrying headlines.

According to a study by the Halle Institute for Economic Research (IWH), the insolvency rate among partnerships and corporations in Germany was 80% higher in June than in an average June between 2016 and 2019, the period before the COVID-19 pandemic.

Sole proprietors, freelancers and very small businesses were not included in these figures because they are considered less relevant to the overall labor market. Partnerships and corporations account for about 90% of jobs affected by insolvencies and 95% of the claims involved.

The number of company bankruptcies in the second quarter of 2026 reached its highest level in 20 years, according to Steffen Müller, head of insolvency research at IWH. This wasn't much of a surprise, since increasing numbers of firms have been going bankrupt for several quarters now.

Industrial job losses on the rise in Germany

Germany's economy has been struggling for years, and the recovery that had been expected for this year is now likely to result in only weak growth. This fits with announcements from major companies about significant job cuts.

Volkswagen has reportedly indicated that up to 100,000 jobs could disappear worldwide over the coming years. Auto supplier ZF plans to eliminate 14,000 positions by 2028.

Bosch also intends to cut more than 20,000 jobs in Germany alone by 2030. But the difficulties are clearly not limited to the automotive industry.

After more than 100,000 industrial jobs were lost last year, a further 100,000 positions could be cut in industry during 2026, according to a study by consulting firm Horvath. The reductions are expected not only in automotive manufacturing, but also in mechanical engineering and construction.

A market correction or structural weakness?

The key question is whether Germany is experiencing a necessary market correction or a deeper structural weakness in its economy.

Insolvencies can have positive effects, too. When unproductive companies leave the market, workers, capital, and expertise become available for more productive sectors. This can promote economic growth through the process that economist Joseph Schumpeter called "creative destruction."

If people who lose their jobs because of insolvencies can quickly find new employment elsewhere, it would suggest a healthy market adjustment rather than economic decline. And in fact, unemployment in Germany has only been rising slowly.

Most people who lose their jobs are able to find new positions.

However, Müller notes that this is partly because many members of the baby-boom generation are now retiring and immigration from within the EU has slowed. As a result, workers are not necessarily moving from less productive companies to more productive ones.

Growing number of startups provides some hope

It is important to look not only at insolvencies but also at the number of newly founded businesses, according to Jutta Rüdlin, a board member of the German Association of Insolvency Administrators and Trustees (VID).

"Young companies fail more often than average, and there has been an increase in the number of startups compared with the previous year," said Rüdlin.

According to the German Federal Statistical Office (Destatis), over 10% more businesses were founded in the first quarter of 2026 than in the same period a year earlier.

"We've actually seen an increase in growth-oriented startups for many years," said Müller.

"That is good news. Many of them are active in the field of artificial intelligence, which gives reason for optimism."

In his view, this suggests that Germany is currently undergoing a structural transformation.

The causes behind insolvencies are diverse

On the other hand, no single sector appears to be bearing the brunt of the problem.

"About a year and a half ago, there were many large insolvencies in manufacturing," said Müller.

But this is no longer the case. This time, the trend is affecting almost all industries, which points more toward a structural weakness in the economy.

Construction and housing development have been hit particularly hard by higher interest rates. Restaurants are struggling with rising minimum wages, energy-intensive industries with increased energy costs, and retailers with changing consumer spending habits.

Rüdlin emphasizes that insolvencies usually do not have one single cause.

In the past, healthy companies have generally proven resilient to external shocks. Major events like the conflict involving the US and Iran tend to act as catalysts for businesses that were already facing fundamental problems, such as outdated business models, delayed management decisions, or a failure to adapt to changing conditions.

The delayed effects of the COVID-19 pandemic

Another factor supporting the market-correction argument is the lingering effect of measures introduced during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Many companies received financial support at that time and must now repay it. Some of these businesses might not have survived even under normal market conditions at the time.

Jutta Rüdlin therefore believes that delayed effects from the COVID-19 era are still playing a role in company insolvencies. In her view, market correction and structural weakness are overlapping phenomena.

IWH researcher Steffen Müller takes a somewhat more drastic view of the situation.

"I think this is more than just a market correction. The real question is what direction the German economy will take in the future," he said, describing insolvency levels as being in the danger zone.

"We are not yet seeing a domino effect in which one company's problems spread to other businesses or banks. But we are in the middle of a major structural transformation."

This article was originally written and published in German.

entries.title: OpenAI says its AI model went rogue and hacked startup
entries.summary: OpenAI called it an "unprecedented cyber incident" and pledged to support a joint investigation, with renewed attention to the risks of advanced artificial intelligence.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

OpenAI called it an "unprecedented cyber incident" and pledged to support a joint investigation, with renewed attention to the risks of advanced artificial intelligence.

OpenAI on Tuesday said that its advanced artificial intelligence models went rogue and hacked into Hugging Face, a digital repository of AI technology.

"We had a significant security incident during evaluation of our models," OpenAI CEO Sam Altman said in a statement posted on social media.

The disclosure comes amid heightened concerns about the cybersecurity risks posed by powerful AI models.

How did the breach happen?

In a blog post, OpenAI said the "unprecedented cyber incident" took place while it was testing the cybersecurity capabilities of its models in a "tightly controlled digital testing ground," where internet access was limited for safety.

It said the AI agent managed to escape containment, reach the internet and break into Hugging Face to try to satisfy its testing goal.

"While operating in our sandboxed testing environment, our models spent a substantial amount of (computing power) finding a way to obtain open Internet access, in pursuit of solving the evaluation problem," an OpenAI blog post about the incident said.

After connecting to the internet, the models decided to target the platform Hugging Face, a large repository of AI models, datasets and other information, to help in their quest.

AI agents are autonomous software systems that carry out tasks in the real world to achieve specific goals.

The AI firm said the incident involved a combination of models, including its recently launched GPT-5.6 Sol "and an even more capable pre-release model."

What did Hugging Face say?

Hugging Face, an open-source AI platform, caused a stir in the cybersecurity community when it said in a blog post last week that it "was different from anything we had handled before" in that "it was driven, end to end, by an autonomous AI agent system."

In a post to X, Hugging Face co-founder Clement Delangue said the company suspected the hack "might have come from a frontier lab, given the sophistication of the agent. Turns out it did!"

He added: "It's quite mind-blowing that all of this happened autonomously!"

AI cybersecurity problems

The disclosure comes amid growing concerns about the cybersecurity risks of advanced AI. In June, President Donald Trump ordered federal reviews of the most powerful AI systems before their public release.

OpenAI's most advanced AI model, GPT-5.6, was launched earlier this month, but only after its debut was delayed at the US government's request over national security fears.

In June, OpenAI rival Anthropic was forced to pull Fable 5 and cybersecurity Mythos model, over concerns that it could pose a security risk by giving hackers and other bad actors access to AI capable of finding and exploiting vulnerabilities exceptionally quickly.

Edited by: Louis Oelofse

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred Source on Google by clicking here  and hitting the "star" or "preferred" button, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Ukraine renews strikes on Russia's largest online retailer
entries.summary: Ukraine's military has again targeted warehouses belonging to Wildberries. The company, which Kyiv says provides logistics for Russia's military, was previously targeted on Saturday.
entries.content:
Ukraine

Ukraine renews strikes on Russia's largest online retailer

July 22, 2026

Ukraine's military has again targeted warehouses belonging to Wildberries. The company, which Kyiv says provides logistics for Russia's military, was previously targeted on Saturday.

Wildberries, Russia's largest online retailer, on Wednesday said that several people were injured in Ukrainian attacks on the company's warehouses.

Vladimir Kondratyev, the governor of southern Krasnodar said one person was killed in Wednesday's Ukraine strikes, but did not clarify whether that was the strikes at Wildberries or elsewhere.

What do we know about the latest Ukrainian attack on Wildberries?

Wildberries co-founder and CEO Tatyana Kim said facilities in Krasnodar and Nevinnomyssk were targeted in Wednesday's attacks.

Social media footage showed smoke billowing above the Krasnodar logistics site, which Kim said had been evacuated along with a facility in Nevinnomyssk.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy addressed the attacks on X, without naming Wildberries.

"Today, Ukraine's long-range sanctions successfully struck important targets in Russia's Krasnodar and Stavropol regions – logistics hubs involved in supplying the Russian army with drone components, navigation equipment, and other gear – as well as another oil depot," Zelenskyy wrote.

Wednesday's attacks follow the deaths of eight employees in strikes that destroyed Wildberries facilities on Saturday.

"There are no ⁠words ​to describe all the feelings and emotions caused by ​the attacks on ordinary people, on our employees, on all of us who are simply doing our ​jobs," Kim said in a ‌post on the Telegram messaging app.

Ukraine expands defense to target Russia's civilian economy

Wildberries, often called the Amazon of Russia, is a key component of the country's civilian economy. Kyiv also accuses it of providing military logistics and shipping for the country's military.

Online sales in Russia totaled 11.5 trillion Rubles ($147 billion, €129 billion) in 2025 according to the country's Association of Internet Trade Companies, a 28% increase over 2024.

Russian Deputy Prime Minister Alexander Novak said sales of goods and services on digital platforms equated to around 8.5% of GDP.

Wednesday's strikes represent a widening trend of Ukrainian attacks deep within Russia as Moscow continues to target civilian infrastructure in the  invasion it launched against neighboring Ukraine on February 24, 2022.

Beyond its expanded attacks on Russia's civilian economy, the commander of Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces announced that Kyiv's military had also struck 13 Russian vessels in the Black Sea and Sea of Azov over the past 48 hours. 

Edited by: Rana Taha

Don't let the algorithm hide the news. If you rely on our team for trusted reporting, please take a moment to select us as your Preferred news source on Google by clicking here, so you'll always see our verified news first.

entries.title: Police station opens in Hitler's birth house
entries.summary: Controversy has surrounded the renovation of the building in Braunau, Austria to this day. The debate points to something larger: the continued need to remember history and confront the Nazi past.
entries.content:
July 22, 2026

Controversy has surrounded the renovation of the building in Braunau, Austria to this day. The debate points to something larger: the continued need to remember history and confront the Nazi past.

Starting Wednesday, July 22, the police in Braunau am Inn, Austria, have new headquarters.

The local police station and district police command have moved into Salzburger Vorstadt 15. The address would attract little attention — had Adolf Hitler not been born there in April 1889.

Hitler, the self-declared "Führer" of the German Reich who started World War II, which claimed tens of millions of lives across Europe and beyond. Hitler also bore primary responsibility for the Holocaust, the systematic murder of approximately six million Jews.

The house didn't belong to Hitler's parents; they were tenants there and moved out only a few months after his birth.

The building has had a turbulent history ever since.

After Nazi Germany annexed Austria in 1938, the Nazi Party purchased the house and turned it into an ideological cultural center dedicated to its leader.

After World War II, ownership returned to the original family. The Austrian government later leased the property, which has served at various times as a library, a school and, most recently, a workshop for people with disabilities.

Starting 2011, the building stood vacant for years.

In 2016, Austria expropriated the property from its private owner to prevent neo-Nazis from turning it into a pilgrimage site. Officials said they wanted to "neutralize" the location.

A €20-million renovation

Austria's Interior Ministry appointed a commission on the historically appropriate use of Hitler's birth house, charging it with developing a concept for the site's future. The commission recommended either a social service organization or a government office.

Oskar Deutsch, president of the Jewish Community of Vienna and member of the commission, told DW in 2023: "The goal was always to ensure this house wouldn't become a pilgrimage site for Nazis." Deutsch said he could have supported other uses for the building. Still, he emphasized that "a police station of a democratic state governed by the rule of law will be located here, and one of its responsibilities is combating neo-Nazi activity."

Historian Oliver Rathkolb, another commission member, recalled that the group's first unanimous recommendation was to house a social welfare organization there — an institution that embraced life. That proposal was declined. "The police station will discourage Hitler admirers from taking selfies in front of the completely redesigned building," Rathkolb wrote.

To that end, the government spent about €20 million ($23 million) completely renovating the building, which included replacing its facade with a deliberately understated design.

Residents of Braunau remain divided over the result.

"A unique historical opportunity was missed to use the birth house of history's greatest mass murderer in a sustainable and historically responsible way," Eveline Doll, a Braunau native and spokesperson for the local citizens' initiative Diskurs Hitlerhaus, told DW. "The city is, of course, not responsible for Hitler's crimes. But around the world, Braunau will always be associated with the fact that history's greatest mass murderer was born here."

A 2023 survey found that only 6% of respondents supported converting the building into a police station.

Police and the Nazi past

"For more than 25 years, people have proposed turning this house into a place dedicated to peace, freedom, democracy and tolerance," Doll said. "Those are values that remain critically important today. But every one of those proposals was rejected, and instead the building became a police station, which raises concerns on several levels."

One of those concerns involves the police's role during the Nazi era. After Hitler appointed Heinrich Himmler chief of the German police in 1936, the police and the SS became increasingly integrated. The Gestapo, the secret state police, emerged as one of the regime's principal instruments of terror and played a central role in persecuting political opponents and carrying out Nazi crimes.

Florian Kotanko, chair of the Contemporary History Society of Braunau, rejects comparisons between the police today and in the Nazi era. "We live in a democratic state governed by the rule of law, and the police are one of its institutions," he told DW. "As long as officers can be held accountable for misconduct and prosecuted in court, there is no reason for fundamental distrust of the police as an institution." Kotanko also takes a pragmatic view of the building's new purpose. "The owner, the Republic of Austria, made its decision," he said.

Austria has wrestled with its Nazi past for decades.

In 1938, Hitler annexed his neighboring homeland into Nazi Germany, a move many Austrians welcomed. During Nazi rule, approximately 65,000 Austrian Jews were murdered and another 130,000 were forced into exile. Critics have repeatedly argued that Austria has not fully acknowledged its share of responsibility for the Holocaust.

The far-right Freedom Party of Austria, founded in 1956 by former Nazis, won the largest share of the 2024 parliamentary electoral vote, but failed to form a government.

A representative 2025 online survey by polling institute Unique Research for Pragmaticus magazine found that 39% of Austrians agreed, or somewhat agreed, that it was time to "draw a line" under confronting the country's Nazi past.

Memorial stone stays

The renovated building will not feature an information plaque. The expert commission also recommended moving the memorial stone in front of the building to another location in Braunau. The stone, taken from Austria's largest concentration camp at Mauthausen, bears the inscription: "For peace, freedom and democracy. Never again fascism. The millions of dead remind us." Local officials rejected that recommendation and left the memorial in place.

Coincidentally, Hitler's last registered address in Germany is also now home to a police facility. The building at Prinzregentenplatz 16 in Munich's Bogenhausen district belonged to Hitler until his death. After World War II, the state of Bavaria used it for various government offices. A police station moved into the building in 1998. The station has recently begun opening its doors for selected public events to demonstrate that democratic values now define a place with such a dark history.

This article was originally written in German.

entries.title: Turkey-PKK talks: Is peace finally possible?
entries.summary: A year ago, the Kurdish militant group PKK announced an end to its armed struggle. Since then, peace talks with Turkey have progressed slowly, but some observers believe they could succeed.
entries.content:
Turkey

Turkey-PKK talks: Is peace finally possible?

July 22, 2026

A year ago, the Kurdish militant group PKK announced an end to its armed struggle. Since then, peace talks with Turkey have progressed slowly, but some observers believe they could succeed.

July marks a year since the Kurdish militant group known as the PKK, listed as a terrorist organization by numerous countries, symbolically burned their weapons.

Last May, the PKK, or Kurdistan Workers' Party, announced it would end its "work under the name of PKK," halting a decades-long armed insurgency between the Turkish state and those fighting for Kurdish rights or independence inside Turkey, one that had cost an estimated 40,000 lives.

Later, on July 11, a group of around 30 PKK fighters in northern Iraq burned their weapons in a symbolic disarmament ceremony.

This year, on that same date, human rights activists, academics and politicians gathered in Diyarbakir, the largest city in Turkey's Kurdish-majority southeast, to assess progress in the ongoing peace process between the Turkish government and the PKK.

The conference was organized by Diyarbakir's administration and the Human Rights Association, or IHD.

One of the IHD's co-chairs, lawyer Cihan Aydin, has proposed making July 11 an official day of remembrance. A vibrant culture of remembrance contributes to coming to terms with the past and prevents future conflicts, he argued. Aydin also called for a legal solution for all those impacted by the peace process, including PKK leader Abdullah Ocalan, who has been jailed by Turkey since 1999.

For decades, Ocalan was considered a terrorist in Turkey. Today the 77-year-old is the focal point of the current peace process. It was he who called on the PKK to lay down its arms in February 2025, a call that members responded to.

Turkey-PKK peace process

Following this, the Turkish parliament established a committee that heard testimony from approximately 140 representatives of politics and civil society, including human rights activists and relatives of soldiers, veterans and prisoners. The committee then recommended a series of reforms to advance the peace process, including the reintegration into society of PKK members who renounced violence, as well as state security institutions verifying that the PKK had genuinely given up its weapons.

Talks have focused on the rules on the return or potential prosecution of PKK members as well as their possible integration into civilian and political life. The Kurds want to see Ocalan released from prison along with other political prisoners.

The Turkish government has not been open to a general amnesty, only a partial one, and has yet to make any concrete commitment regarding Ocalan. However, more recently the country's ruling party, AKP, seems to have struck a more moderate tone.

This makes Aret Demirci, head of the Istanbul office of Germany's Friedrich Naumann Foundation, believe that progress in the talks should be seen as "cautiously positive."

The fact that the PKK symbolically burned their weapons marked an "historic turning point," he says. "And unlike previous peace initiatives, the process has not collapsed at an early stage."

But, Demirci cautions, so far the central political questions have mostly been ignored. The Turkish government is pushing for the PKK's complete disarmament while the Kurdish side wants political and legal guarantees before that happens. "The parties' differing expectations are likely to be the biggest challenge in coming months," Demirci notes.

After meeting with Ocalan and others in prison on Monday this week, pro-Kurdish political party DEM released a statement in which Ocalan repeated his call for a legal framework to advance the peace process. "The Kurdish-Turkish alliance is the cornerstone of Anatolia's shared future," Ocalan stressed in the statement.

The PKK's own leadership also seems optimistic. Duran Kalkan, one of the PKK's co-founders, said in an interview this weekend that the peace process wouldn't fail because the situation in the region is so different this time.

Changing security situation for Turkey

Many observers share Kalkan's opinion. Conflicts in the Middle East have intensified recently, especially due to tensions between Israel, Iran and the US. The changes in Syria are also important.

Turkey faces a totally different security situation than it did 10 years ago, when informal talks between Turkish intelligence agencies and the PKK, that began in 2009, evolved into negotiations between 2013 and 2015. In March 2013, Ocalan had also declared a ceasefire, although this didn't last.

But Demirci thinks that geopolitical changes are forcing dialogue between the PKK and Turkey to move forward.

The new government in Syria plays a key role. Having a better relationship with Kurds inside Turkey gives the Turkish government greater leverage regarding Syrian Kurds and Iraqi Kurds. The picture has also changed for the PKK and its affiliates.

The Turkish have been worried that PKK members might join Iranian-Kurdish militias over the border. And previously, during the Syrian civil war, Syrian Kurdish groups, allegedly associated with the PKK and supported by the US, had been semi-independent and controlled a large area almost directly on the Turkish border. This also concerned Turkish security agencies immensely. But now Syria's Kurds have had to find an accommodation with the new Syrian government and are no longer in complete charge of this area.

How successful the peace process is in the longer term won't depend only on the end of violence though, Demirci told DW. It also depends on whether a political dialogue emerges that encompasses democratic reforms, the rule of law and more general questions about Kurdish identity in Turkey.

As long as opposition politicians are arrested, Kurdish mayors removed from office and political freedoms restricted, the end of armed conflict alone won't lead to genuine, sustainable peace.

"This very ambivalence currently shapes the debate in Turkey and makes the process both historic and extremely fragile," he says.

A lack of transparency is also problematic. "There is hardly any public information about which agreements have actually been reached and what steps are planned," Demirci continued. "That makes it difficult to build public trust."

With additional reporting by Gülsen Solaker.

This story was originally published in German.
